content.text: is the fundamental base class for all neural networks in PyTorch. It provides a flexible framework to define and manage neural network components, enabling creation of complex models efficiently.
(fully connected, convolutional, activation functions) inherits from
. By subclassing it, you define your own models and layers with customized behavior and structure.
is essential because it provides five critical capabilities:
Hierarchical nesting of layers (Linear, Conv2d, activations)
Build complex models like GANs, diffusion models, LLMs
Automatically tracks all learnable parameters (weights, biases)
moves model + all parameters to GPU
GPU acceleration for training large models
It simplifies neural network design, layer definition, weight initialization, and forward propagation.
All custom neural networks follow this pattern:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class NeuralNetwork(nn.Module):  # Subclass nn.Module [web:24]
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()  # Initialize base class [web:24]
        # Define layers here (automatically registered as parameters)
        self.layer1 = nn.Linear(128, 256)  # Parameters tracked [web:23]
        self.layer2 = nn.Linear(256, 784)
        self.relu = nn.ReLU()
    
    def forward(self, x):  # Define forward pass [web:22]
        x = self.relu(self.layer1(x))  # Apply layers [web:24]
        x = self.layer2(x)
        return x  # Return output [web:24]

# Usage
model = NeuralNetwork()  # Create instance
input_tensor = torch.randn(32, 128)  # Batch of 32 samples
output = model(input_tensor)  # Calls forward() automatically [web:21]

model = NeuralNetwork()

# All parameters are tensors with gradient tracking
for param in model.parameters():
    print(param.shape)      # Tensor shape
    print(param.requires_grad)  # True (Autograd enabled) [web:25]
Parameters are automatically registered and tracked. When you call
, it collects all parameters from the model and submodules.

model = NeuralNetwork()
input_tensor = torch.randn(32, 128)

# Forward pass builds computational graph via Autograd
output = model(input_tensor)  
loss = output.mean()

# Backward pass computes gradients for ALL parameters automatically
loss.backward()  # Gradients stored in param.grad [web:25]

# Optimizer uses gradients to update weights
for param in model.parameters():
    param -= 0.01 * param.grad  # Gradient descent [web:25]
makes gradient computation and weight updates automatic.
: Linear, Conv, ReLU are pre-built blocks
: Output of one feeds input of next
: PyTorch knows all blocks and their parameters
: Data flows through blocks in order
: Gradients flow backward through same path
You're building a factory where data flows through stations (layers), and
The following diagram shows the four-phase lifecycle of a PyTorch training loop as mediated by nn.Module, plus the internal bookkeeping structures that make parameter management automatic.

attrs.alt: nn.Module lifecycle: define → move to device → forward pass → backward + update, with parameter registry and hierarchical module tree

content.text: Use nn.Module whenever you are building any trainable component in PyTorch. The class is designed to be the universal container — there is no situation where you would want to bypass it for a trainable network. The decision is not whether to use nn.Module but how to structure the module hierarchy.

Use a flat subclass (single nn.Module with all layers in __init__) for small networks under ~5 layers where the architecture is fixed. Use nested modules — a module that contains other nn.Module instances as attributes — when you have logical components (encoder/decoder, generator/discriminator) that may be trained separately or reused. Use nn.Sequential when the computation is a simple chain with no branching or conditional logic.
Any trainable model — classifiers, generative models, custom layers, loss networks.
When you need state (buffers, running statistics, cached tensors) alongside parameters.
When you need to save/load weights, apply to GPU, or hand parameters to an optimizer.
Pure functional transformations with no learnable weights (use torch.nn.functional directly).
Pre-processing or post-processing code that never participates in gradient computation.
Assign any nn.Parameter or child nn.Module as an attribute and it is tracked automatically. Call model.parameters() once to get every leaf tensor the optimizer needs to update.
Modules nest cleanly. A Transformer is a module containing TransformerLayer modules containing MultiheadAttention and FeedForward modules. Each level is independently testable and reusable.
.to(device, dtype) recurses the whole tree. Switch from CPU to GPU or from float32 to bfloat16 in one call.
model.train() and model.eval() propagate to all child modules, controlling dropout and BatchNorm behaviour with a single call.
Register forward hooks, backward hooks, and forward pre-hooks to inspect or modify activations during training without editing model code.

The __call__ machinery (hooks, grad mode checks) adds microseconds per call. For extremely tight inner loops with tiny operations, this overhead can be measurable. torch.compile() largely eliminates it.
If your forward() mutates module state (self.counter += 1), the computation graph will capture this mutation, which may cause issues with gradient checkpointing or torch.jit.script.
Saving a full model with torch.save(model) pickles the class definition. If you move the file, the class must be importable. Always prefer state_dict for portability.
Dynamic shapes require care with compilation:
torch.compile traces shapes at first call. Variable sequence lengths or dynamic control flow in forward() trigger retraces or require mark_dynamic annotations.
Without this, nn.Module's internal dictionaries are not initialised and every attribute assignment will raise AttributeError.
Use nn.ModuleList and nn.ModuleDict for collections:
Plain Python lists and dicts are invisible to PyTorch's parameter tracking. nn.ModuleList and nn.ModuleDict ensure all child modules are registered.
If two parts of a model are independently meaningful (encoder/decoder), put them in separate nn.Module subclasses. This makes unit testing, weight loading, and fine-tuning far easier.
eval() fixes batch norm and dropout; no_grad() prevents gradient tape allocation, reducing memory and speeding inference by ~20–30%.
Save state_dict, not the whole model:
torch.save(model.state_dict(), path) is portable across Python versions and does not depend on the class being importable at load time.
Define an init function and call model.apply(init_fn). It recurses the module tree, visiting every leaf module.

A Variational Autoencoder illustrates how nn.Module handles a non-trivial model with multiple subnetworks, custom forward logic (the reparameterization trick), and multiple loss terms. This is the pattern used in production generative modelling pipelines.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F


class Encoder(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim: int, hidden_dim: int, latent_dim: int):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc_mu     = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, latent_dim)
        self.fc_logvar = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, latent_dim)

    def forward(self, x):
        h = F.relu(self.fc1(x))
        return self.fc_mu(h), self.fc_logvar(h)  # mean and log-variance


class Decoder(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, latent_dim: int, hidden_dim: int, output_dim: int):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1  = nn.Linear(latent_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc_out = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, output_dim)

    def forward(self, z):
        h = F.relu(self.fc1(z))
        return self.fc_out(h)  # logits for BCE


class VAE(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim=784, hidden_dim=256, latent_dim=20):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = Encoder(input_dim, hidden_dim, latent_dim)
        self.decoder = Decoder(latent_dim, hidden_dim, input_dim)

    def reparameterise(self, mu, log_var):
        std = torch.exp(0.5 * log_var)
        eps = torch.randn_like(std)      # sample from N(0,I)
        return mu + eps * std

    def forward(self, x):
        mu, log_var = self.encoder(x)
        z = self.reparameterise(mu, log_var)
        x_recon = self.decoder(z)
        return x_recon, mu, log_var

    def loss(self, x, x_recon, mu, log_var, beta=1.0):
        recon = F.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(
            x_recon, x, reduction='sum') / x.size(0)
        kl = -0.5 * (1 + log_var - mu.pow(2) - log_var.exp()).sum(1).mean()
        return recon + beta * kl


# ---- Training loop ----
device = 'cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu'
vae = VAE().to(device)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(vae.parameters(), lr=1e-3)

# Simulated training step
vae.train()
for step in range(3):   # replace with real dataloader
    x = torch.rand(64, 784).to(device)   # fake batch
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    x_recon, mu, log_var = vae(x)
    loss = vae.loss(x, x_recon, mu, log_var)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    print(f'step {step}: loss={loss.item():.2f}')

# Save
torch.save(vae.state_dict(), 'vae.pt')

# Inference
vae.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    z = torch.randn(4, 20).to(device)
    samples = torch.sigmoid(vae.decoder(z))  # generated images
    print('sample shape:', samples.shape)    # (4, 784)

nn.Module introduces negligible overhead for typical models. A forward pass through a 10-layer MLP incurs fewer than 10 µs of Python overhead — dwarfed by GPU kernel launch latency (~50–200 µs per CUDA kernel). Three optimisations are worth knowing:

Wraps the model in an optimising compiler that fuses operations, eliminates Python overhead, and generates efficient CUDA kernels. Typically 1.5–3× throughput improvement on modern GPUs with a single decorator: model = torch.compile(model).

torch.utils.checkpoint.checkpoint(module, *inputs) recomputes activations during the backward pass instead of storing them. Reduces activation memory by O(sqrt(L)) for L layers, at the cost of ~33% extra compute.

nn.DataParallel wraps any module to replicate it across GPUs within a single process. torch.nn.parallel.DistributedDataParallel is the production choice for multi-node training — it wraps any nn.Module and handles gradient synchronisation.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# torch.compile for ~2x throughput on A100
model = VAE().to('cuda')
model = torch.compile(model)   # JIT compile the forward pass

# Gradient checkpointing to halve activation memory
from torch.utils.checkpoint import checkpoint

class CheckpointedVAE(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = Encoder(784, 256, 20)
        self.decoder = Decoder(20, 256, 784)

    def forward(self, x):
        # Recompute encoder activations during backward instead of storing them
        mu, log_var = checkpoint(self.encoder, x, use_reentrant=False)
        z = self.reparameterise(mu, log_var)
        return checkpoint(self.decoder, z, use_reentrant=False), mu, log_var
Print model summary with per-layer shapes and parameter counts
Training loop scaffolding built on top of nn.Module
Wrap any nn.Module with LoRA / adapter layers without modifying original code
Export nn.Module to a serialisable IR for deployment without Python
for all neural networks in PyTorch
: Used in GANs, diffusion models, LLMs, transformers
provides the structured foundation for building, managing, and deploying deep learning models, making PyTorch both powerful and maintainable for production generative AI systems.
